title: React Fragments Quiz
description: Evaluate your understanding of DOM cleanliness, the long vs short syntax, and why avoiding extra divs matters.

questions.question: Which and/or what is the primary purpose of a React Fragment?
options: To group multiple React elements without adding extra nodes to the DOM tree.
To speed up the app by caching components.
To provide a replacement for CSS styles.

questions.question: Which of the following is the 'short' syntax for declaring a React Fragment?
Why would you use a Fragment instead of a <div> for grouping elements?
options: To avoid cluttering the DOM with extra structural nodes.
To ensure that CSS grid or flexbox layouts behave correctly with expected children.
To improve performance by minimizing DOM node count.

questions.question: Can you use a 'key' prop with the short Fragment syntax (<>...</>)?
options: No, you must use the full <React.Fragment key={id}> syntax if you need a key.
Only in older versions of React.

questions.question: Does using a Fragment impact the component tree structure for React's own internal diffing engine?
options: Yes, it creates a new parent-child depth level.
No, React treats children of a Fragment as direct siblings in its internal logic.
Only if you use the short syntax.
Only if you return them with an ID.
